Engineering Portfolio · Simulation & Structural Dynamics

Simulation of Vibration Time Series for the Development of Structural Health Monitoring Algorithms

From high-fidelity FEM to fast reduced-order models — generating synthetic vibration data for SHM
Janmesh Thakare
University of Siegen
Dept. of Mechanical Engineering
Structural Health Monitoring
Studienarbeit · Oct 17, 2024
Supervisors Prof. Dr.-Ing. Peter Kraemer · M.Sc. Tobias Adam Tools COMSOL Multiphysics 6.1 · LiveLink for MATLAB · sssMOR
COMSOL 6.1MATLAB LiveLinkFEM Modal AnalysisModel Order ReductionsssMORSHM

01Project Summary

Structural Health Monitoring (SHM) algorithms need vibration data — lots of it, under controlled and repeatable conditions that physical testing cannot always provide. This project built a complete simulation pipeline generating physically realistic vibration time series of a simply supported beam in COMSOL Multiphysics 6.1 coupled to MATLAB via LiveLink. Two independent finite element formulations — a 1D Timoshenko Beam Physics model and a full 3D Solid Mechanics model — were cross-validated through eigenfrequency analysis, then driven with time-varying multi-axis loads to produce displacement time histories. Model Order Reduction with the sssMOR toolbox compressed the full state-space systems into compact reduced models that reproduce the full-order dynamics while cutting computation time by 60–67% — a scalable approach for generating large SHM training datasets.

8 + 8
Eigenfrequencies validated · COMSOL ↔ MATLAB, two physics models
0.077%
Beam-theory vs. 3D-elasticity gap at mode 1 (5.5% at mode 8)
−67%
Computation time with ROM, at full-order displacement accuracy

02Technical Objectives

  • Build FEM models in two COMSOL interfaces: Beam Physics (Hermitian / Timoshenko elements) and Solid Mechanics (3D FreeTet continuum).
  • Compute the first 8 eigenfrequencies and mode shapes; validate against MATLAB via LiveLink.
  • Quantify beam-theory vs. 3D-elasticity divergence across the modal spectrum.
  • Run transient simulations under 3-axis time-varying point loads (pulse, sinusoid, smoothed step).
  • Extract state-space matrices (A, B, C, D) and apply modal order reduction with sssMOR.
  • Benchmark accuracy vs. computational cost; generate 0–300 ms vibration time series for SHM development.

03Theory in One Glance

Discretized structural dynamics are governed by the equation of motion; natural frequencies follow from the undamped eigenvalue problem:

M·ü(t) + C·u̇(t) + K·u(t) = F(t)
det[ K − ω² M ] = 0

Modal reduction projects the full system onto its dominant modes (m ≪ n DOF). The first 8 eigenfrequencies were sufficient to resolve the dynamic response to the applied load spectrum.

04Simulation Setup

Geometry · Material · Boundary Conditions
ParameterValue
Beam (L × W × H)10 × 0.5 × 0.25 m
Cross sectionRect., hy = 0.5 m, hz = 0.25 m
Material (steel-like)E = 200 GPa, ν = 0.3, ρ = 8000 kg/m³
Boundary conditionsSimply supported (fixed ends)
Rayleigh dampingαdM = 50, βdK = 1×10⁻⁴
Time stepping0.25 ms · 0–25 ms / 0–300 ms
Applied forces at beam center (L/2, W/2, H)
AxisForce component
X400 N × rect1(t), active 4–4.5 ms
Y500 N × sin(2π·300 Hz·t)
Z−100 N × step1(t), 2 ms transition

Eigenfrequency-stage load set: Fx = 100 N·sin(2π·200 Hz·t), Fy = −11,000 N static, Fz = 700 N·sin(2π·100 Hz·t). Geometry, cross-section and model views: Figs. A1–A3.

Janmesh Thakare — SHM Vibration Simulation PortfolioPage 1 / 7

05Two Physics Models, One Beam

AspectBeam Physics modelSolid Mechanics model
Geometry / elements1D line + analytical cross-section · Hermitian beam elements3D block · free tetrahedral (FreeTet) continuum
Mesh controlEdge distribution (8 elements) + sweep, hmax = 8 mmhauto = 4
Theory / trade-offTimoshenko assumptions — tiny system, constrained kinematicsFull 3D elasticity — no kinematic assumptions, larger matrices

The dual-model architecture is built-in verification: agreement at low modes confirms both implementations; divergence at high modes quantifies exactly where Timoshenko assumptions (planar cross-sections, no warping or local distortion) break down against the full elasticity solution. Mode shapes: Figs. A4–A5.

ModeCOMSOL (Hz)MATLAB LiveLink (Hz)
112.9612.95
225.4825.48
335.5935.56
468.7768.77
569.4469.36
6114.09113.95
7121.72121.47
8131.30131.31

·Eigenfrequencies — Beam Physics

ModeCOMSOL (Hz)MATLAB LiveLink (Hz)
112.8412.86
225.7025.71
335.4235.42
469.4469.44
570.8471.02
6114.81114.11
7121.37115.42
8138.88138.95

Cross-model agreement is near-perfect at the fundamental mode — 0.10 Hz (0.077%) — and degrades progressively to a maximum of 7.65 Hz (5.5%) at mode 8, as shortening modal wavelengths expose the simplified geometric, kinematic and constitutive assumptions of beam theory plus idealized boundary conditions. COMSOL ↔ MATLAB agreement within each model (typically < 0.1 Hz) validates the scripted automation pipeline itself. Eigenfrequency plots: Figs. A6–A7.

07Reduced-Order Modelling with sssMOR

1 · DEFINE I/OForces as COMSOL parameters (inputs); center-point displacement probes (outputs)
2 · EXTRACTmphstate exports sparse A, B, C, D to MATLAB
3 · WRAPsssMOR sss object handles large sparse matrices
4 · REDUCEModal projection → compact Am, Bm, Cm, Dm
5 · BENCHMARKSimulate in MATLAB vs. full-order reference
%% Extraction & reduction — from the actual LiveLink script
M = mphstate(model, 'sol1', 'out',{'Mc' 'MA' 'MB' 'C' 'D' 'x0','Null','ud'}, ...
    'input',{'F_x' 'F_y' 'F_z'}, 'output',{'comp1.dom1','comp1.dom2','comp1.dom3'}, 'sparse','on');
Em=M.Mc; Am=M.MA; Bm=M.MB; Cm=M.C; Dm=M.D;
sys1_sss = sss(Am,Bm,Cm,Dm,Em);                  % sparse state-space system
sys_r = modalMor(sys1_sss, 16);                   % modal order reduction, order = 16
frequencies_ur = unique(abs(imag(eigs(A,E,16)) / (2*pi)));   % verify eigenfrequencies

Reduced-order displacement traces lie essentially on top of the full-order references on all three axes (Figs. A8–A10): X near zero, Y oscillatory in-plane, Z carrying the dominant out-of-plane deflection to ≈ −0.0026 mm peak.

Janmesh Thakare — SHM Vibration Simulation PortfolioPage 2 / 7

08Time Series & Stress Behaviour

The Z-component dominates the response. After a transient of large oscillatory swings — the step and pulse loads exciting the lower bending modes — Rayleigh damping dissipates the vibrational energy and the system settles into steady state around 150–300 ms, with the solid beam's Z-displacement converging to ≈ −0.002 mm (Figs. A11–A14).

At peak loading (t = 25 ms) the von Mises stress reaches ≈ 45 × 10⁻³ MPa, concentrated at mid-span — exactly where bending theory places the maximum moment for a simply supported beam. By 300 ms the field has relaxed to a uniform low-stress distribution; XY / XZ / YZ plane views confirm symmetric, bending-consistent deformation with sinusoidal nodal-line interference patterns (Figs. A15–A19).

The result is exactly what SHM development requires: clean, labeled, repeatable displacement histories with known excitation, from which velocity and acceleration can be derived — and into which damage scenarios can later be injected by perturbing stiffness or boundary conditions, re-running the now-cheap reduced model.

09Computational Performance

ModelUnreduced (s)Reduced (s)Speedup
Solid Mechanics93−67%
Beam Physics104−60%

Per run the saving is seconds; across the thousands of runs SHM dataset generation requires (load cases × damage states × noise realizations), it is the difference between hours and days of compute. The trade-off — truncation of high-order modes outside the ≤ 300 Hz excitation bandwidth — is negligible, since the first 8 modes fully resolve the response.

10Code Architecture

  • bracket_eigenfrequency_Slenderbeam.m — builds the 1D Beam Physics model: line geometry, cross-section, constraints, edge meshing, eigenfrequency study.
  • SlenderBeam_Eigenfrequency.m — parametrized forces; LiveLink extraction of natural frequencies for COMSOL comparison.
  • SolidMechanics_beam_eigenfrequencies.m — 3D block, FreeTet mesh, point load (Fx, Fy, Fz), modal participation factors.
  • beam_solidMechanics_matlab_MOR.m / ROM_beam_Solidmechanics_working.m — transient studies, mphstate extraction, sssMOR reduction, time series generation.

11Key Findings

  • COMSOL and MATLAB LiveLink agree to ~0.1 Hz on all eigenfrequencies — the scripted pipeline is validated end-to-end.
  • Beam theory matches 3D elasticity to 0.077% at mode 1, diverging to 5.5% at mode 8: a quantified map of where Timoshenko assumptions break down.
  • 8 eigenfrequencies suffice to resolve the response to the applied multi-axis load spectrum.
  • sssMOR reduction reproduces full-order displacement with no visually distinguishable error.
  • Computation time drops 67% (Solid Mechanics) / 60% (Beam Physics) — decisive for large-scale dataset generation.
  • Z-dominated response: peak ≈ −0.0026 mm, steady state ≈ −0.002 mm by 150–300 ms.
  • Mid-span stress concentration (~45×10⁻³ MPa at 25 ms) relaxes to a uniform low-stress field at 300 ms — consistent with simply supported bending.
  • 0–300 ms series at 0.25 ms resolution: a directly usable, extensible SHM benchmarking dataset.
